original_abstract: This paper proposes an innovative interpretative framework for analyzing the competitiveness of tourism destinations that leverage both tangible and intangible cultural heritage alongside local creative industries. By synthesizing Italian and international literature on destination management, the authors examine how the intersection of traditional cultural assets and modern "content industries"—such as design, architecture, and digital media—drives regional development. The research transitions from a traditional focus on conservation to a more dynamic model of "creative tourism," where the territory serves as a fertile ground for human capital and social innovation. Using data from the European and Italian contexts, the study highlights the significant multiplier effects of cultural production on the broader economy. The resulting three-axis model—comprising territorial attractions, tourism infrastructure, and the system of culture and creativity—provides a strategic tool for policy makers and stakeholders. The paper concludes by discussing the challenges of governance and the need for integrated management strategies to enhance the sustainable well-being of local residents while maintaining competitive differentiation in a globalized market.
humanized_abstract: This paper presents a new interpretative key for assessing the competitiveness of a tourism destination through the means of its tangible and intangible cultural assets and local creative industries. Through a review of the literature concerning destination management, from an Italian and international perspective, the authors analyze the relations between cultural heritage and “content industries” such as design, architecture and digital media and the ways in which these relationships can support the economic development of a territory. Starting from a context in which the conservation of cultural heritage was often the prevalent focus, the paper passes into a perspective on “creative tourism” in which the territory works as an human capital and as a hub for social innovation. Through the analysis of the multipliers and of the economic impact on the context in which they operate, based on international and national case studies in Europe and Italy, the paper finally illustrates a three-axis model (territorial attractions, tourism infrastructure and the system of culture and creativity) and the potentialities and challenges it presents for managers and policymakers that have to promote the sustainable well-being of local communities while at the same time ensuring the competitiveness of each destination in the global market.
